# Questions in Livestream 207

Live Stream #207: Root of the Problem

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=695" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">11:35</a> | Did you really set up an affiliate link to your own book? | Yes. He said it does not raise the buyer's price and helps recover margin that would otherwise go to Amazon. | He explained that the affiliate link leaves the customer's price unchanged, while Amazon's changed discount structure had reduced author margin on print books. The link lets him recover a small extra amount that would otherwise remain with Amazon, so he framed it as a response to the platform's economics rather than an added charge to readers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=1022" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:02</a> | Have you tried getting your books into bookstores? | He said that is very difficult without big-publisher distribution and access to the locked-up retail channel. | He said major bookstore access is largely controlled by the major publishers, so independent authors have little leverage in that retail system. Instead of cancelling orders, he urged readers to leave reviews on Amazon, Goodreads, or wherever they bought the book, because reviews are what help generate additional sales. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=1169" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:29</a> | How much is the ebook version? | He said it is $5.50 and that Amazon takes a larger percentage of ebook revenue than print revenue. | He gave the ebook price as $5.50, then noted that Amazon takes a larger share from the ebook than from the print formats. In his breakdown, the ebook leaves him less revenue than the softcover, which he used as another example of why the publishing economics are frustrating. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=1275" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">21:15</a> | Have you considered signed copies? | He has, but shipping from Canada makes them prohibitively expensive; signed book plates are the more feasible option. | He said signed copies are possible in theory, but Canada Post shipping and the cost of buying and reshipping the books make the numbers unworkable, especially to the United States. He described signed book plates as the practical alternative because they can be mailed cheaply and placed inside a reader's copy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=1566" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">26:06</a> | Did Moses's staff transform into a crocodile in Pharaoh's palace? | He said the text says a serpent-like creature, not clearly a crocodile, and personally thinks a Nile monitor is more plausible. | He said the biblical term can refer broadly to a snake or lizard and should not be forced into the crocodile interpretation. Because the creature eats the magicians' serpents, he argued a Nile monitor fits the scene better than either a crocodile or an ordinary snake, since monitors are fast, native to the Nile region, and eat snakes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=1709" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:29</a> | Did Egyptians practice transformation magic like that? | He said Egyptian texts do preserve transformation stories, including wax-crocodile material. | He pointed to Egyptian story material, probably Papyrus Westcar, in which a magician uses a wax crocodile that becomes a real crocodile. He treated this as evidence that transformation magic belonged in the Egyptian imaginative world, while also noting the story's odd moral framing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=1857" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:57</a> | Did Samson get his strength from his hair, from the Lord, or both? | He said the power came from the Lord, with Samson's hair functioning as part of the Nazirite sign tied to that calling. | He read Judges 16 as saying explicitly that Samson's strength left because the Lord departed from him. The hair mattered as one provision of the Nazirite vow, but the power itself was God's blessing tied to Samson's consecration, and Samson had already violated the vow's provisions in more than one way. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=2116" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">35:16</a> | How likely is the tradition that Thomas traveled to India to preach the gospel? | He said the tradition is more plausible than many assume because names once doubted in the account later received archaeological support. | He described the Thomas-in-India tradition as once considered one of the least likely apostolic travel accounts because names in the story were doubted. He said later coin finds bearing those royal names changed scholarly opinion, making the tradition much more credible among patristic scholars. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=2265" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:45</a> | Was God's presence in the Second Temple? | Yes. He argued that the tearing of the veil at Christ's crucifixion indicates God's presence was there. | He reasoned from the temple-presence pattern: God's glory filled the Holy of Holies, and the veil's tearing at Christ's crucifixion signifies that presence leaving the Holy of Holies and entering the world. For him, that event functions as evidence that the divine presence was truly in the Second Temple. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=2425" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">40:25</a> | Is the tearing of the temple veil related to tearing clothing? | Yes in the sense that the veil was cloth; he also noted that temple veils were not unique to Israelite temples. | He answered that the connection works at least materially, because the temple veil was a cloth veil around the Holy of Holies. He also broadened the context by noting that other ancient temples, including Egyptian ones, used veils around sacred spaces. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=2486" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:26</a> | If you wrote an Exodus commentary, what would your funding goal be? | He said funding is no longer the main obstacle; publishing and marketing an independent commentary would be the harder problem. | He contrasted the earlier need for a large funding goal with his current situation, saying channel income means he would not need the same kind of subsistence funding. The real difficulty would be that commentaries sell best as part of established publisher series, and independent commentaries are hard to market without institutional and publisher access. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=2774" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">46:14</a> | What is a parish? | He defined it as a district overseen by a church authority, and noted the term can also be used for political districts. | He defined a parish as a territorial district under a pastoral authority, especially in large church bodies such as Roman Catholicism. He added that the same word can be used politically, as in Louisiana, but the basic idea remains a local district under an overseeing authority. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=2945" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">49:05</a> | What is something you wish people knew more about the crucifixion and Christ's passion? | He emphasized that nails really were used, including with wooden washers, and cited archaeological evidence from a crucified man's ankle bone. | He pushed back on the claim that crucifixion victims were merely tied up, pointing to the Gospel reference to nail scars and to archaeological evidence from a crucified ankle bone with a nail still present. He also explained that Romans used wooden washers so the nail would hold the body in place rather than tearing through. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=3121" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:01</a> | What does "It is finished" mean? | He said it points to a purpose completed and a debt paid, not merely that the ordeal was over. | He connected the saying to the Greek term associated with completion, purpose, and paid debts. In his explanation, Jesus was not simply saying the suffering had ended, but that the crucifixion had accomplished its intended purpose: the payment for sins. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=3249" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:09</a> | Does "It is finished" connect to the Thummim? | No. He said Thummim is a separate Hebrew term with its own uncertainties. | He rejected a connection because Thummim is a Hebrew word from the Urim and Thummim tradition, not the Greek term behind "It is finished." He also noted that Thummim is itself controversial and lacks clear Semitic analogues, so it should not be used to explain the crucifixion saying. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=3285" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:45</a> | If we saw Eve in the garden, what would Satan have looked like? | He said Satan would be an angelic or spiritual being, but human perception may shape whether he appeared serpentine or otherwise. | He said Satan would be a spiritual or angelic being, but what a human observer perceived could vary because the mind fills in what it expects when encountering a spirit. Eve might have perceived something serpentine, while another observer might perceive imagery more like Ezekiel's angelic beings or something else entirely. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=3455" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:35</a> | What do you think of Dr. Siguad's model of the Trinity? | He said it is a valiant attempt but skirts modalism and does not successfully resolve the Trinitarian issue. | He described the model as one divine entity expressed through distinct tropes of Father, Son, and Spirit. His concern was that this makes one essence play different roles without truly grounding three hypostases, so it comes too close to modalism while trying to avoid partialism. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=3639" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:00:39</a> | Is modalism a heresy? | Yes. He said modalism is absolutely a heresy. | He said orthodox Trinitarianism confesses three persons as the one God, not one personality adopting three roles. Modalism fails because it collapses Father, Son, and Spirit into role-playing by a single essence rather than maintaining eternally distinct persons. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=3705" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:01:45</a> | What do you think of William Lane Craig's Cerberus analogy? | He said it is a weak analogy that risks moving toward partialism. | He treated the one-dog, three-heads analogy as inadequate because it tends to divide the Trinity into parts of a composite creature. He used it as an example of why Trinitarian analogies often fail and can push people toward heretical categories. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=3793" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:03:13</a> | Is monarchial trinitarianism heresy or orthodoxy? | It can be orthodox if it means one God in three persons, but the terminology must be used carefully. | He said monarchial trinitarianism can be orthodox when it means one God in three persons and does not become tritheism. The answer depends on definitions, because related phrases can slide toward social trinitarianism or other models if the relations among the persons are not stated carefully. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=3893" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:04:53</a> | Is only the Father God, or are the three persons the one God? | He said the three persons are the one God, with later church language reconciling Father, Son, and Spirit with divine unity. | He answered that the three persons are the one God, while noting that New Testament usage often uses "God" especially for the Father. The later church had to reconcile the Father's divine identity with the Son and Spirit also being divine, leading to doctrines such as eternal generation and shared divine essence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=4190" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:09:50</a> | Is the Trinity a logical necessity? | He said it helps answer how God can be good before creation, because goodness can be expressed within the Trinity. | He framed the issue philosophically: if God existed alone before creation, one must ask how divine goodness was expressed. The Trinity answers that by locating goodness in the eternal relations among Father, Son, and Spirit before any created recipient existed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=4253" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:10:53</a> | Why is the Athanasian Creed not authoritative for Orthodox Christians? | He said Eastern Orthodox Christians do hold to it as part of tradition, though they do not recite it and interpret it through eternal generation. | He said the Athanasian Creed is part of Christian tradition for Eastern Orthodox Christians, even though it is not normally recited in their churches. He added that Orthodox interpretation tends to read it through eternal generation, and he connected Athanasius's language to the political and theological context of semi-Arian debates. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=4818" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:20:18</a> | Do Terah leaving Ur and Abraham fighting Hammurabi make sense within the biblical narrative? | He said the events are close in time, but not necessarily in the same order, and the date window may not be as straightforward as it appears. | He said Terah leaving Ur would fall in the first half of Hammurabi's reign, while Abraham fighting Hammurabi would be later, so the events are close. He cautioned that the biblical chronology is not always as simple as a calendar reconstruction, especially with the length of Abraham's stay in Haran. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=4920" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:22:00</a> | Is adoptionistic Christology anachronistic? | He said adoptionism is a heresy that leads toward low Christology and a kind of created lower deity. | He did not focus on anachronism so much as on the theological result. In his account, adoptionism treats Christ as a fully human person later adopted and deified by God, which produces a low Christology, tends toward ditheism or Arianism, and resembles Greek apotheosis. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=5036" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:23:56</a> | Why is the Old Testament so underrated? | He said many readers approach it too simplistically, often through modern Marcionite assumptions about wrath and violence. | He said people often read the Old Testament through a simplistic lens that contrasts an angry Old Testament God with a loving New Testament God. He rejected that as a modern form of Marcionite thinking, emphasizing that the Old Testament also presents God as loving and gracious and that the New Testament contains divine wrath as well. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=5264" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:27:44</a> | What does Deuteronomy 12:15 mean? | He said the passage allows ordinary slaughter and eating within one's gates, with clean and unclean referring to people rather than animals. | He argued that "the clean and the unclean may eat of it" refers to ritually clean and unclean people, not clean and unclean animals. The point is that ordinary household slaughter for food within one's gates is permitted, and both ritually clean and unclean persons may eat that non-sacrificial food. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=5438" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:38</a> | How are "begotten of" and "proceeding from" different? | He said the terms overlap but differ in nuance: begotten implies relationship, while proceeding can involve being sent or a procession of substance. | He said "begotten" often involves procreation language but can also describe indirect relationship, such as a grandfather begetting a grandson. "Proceeding" can refer either to being sent or to a procession of substance, so the meanings overlap but depend on context rather than being identical technical terms. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=5638" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:33:58</a> | What are your thoughts on the filioque? | He said the Orthodox are right that the filioque is wrong, but for the wrong reasons; he grounds the issue in the Father sending the Spirit at the Son's behest. | He summarized the filioque as saying the Holy Spirit proceeds from the Father and the Son. He said the Orthodox objection is right in outcome but not in its eternal-generation reasoning; his own reason is that the text presents the Father as sending the Comforter at the Son's request. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=5826" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:37:06</a> | How do you water seed with your feet in Deuteronomy 11:10? | He said Egyptian irrigation used canals and small channels made with the feet to move water to crops. | He contrasted Egypt with the rain-fed Levant. In Egypt, farmers relied on Nile-fed canals and then used their feet to stamp or open small channels that moved water from the canals into crop rows, which is the practice he sees behind the verse. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=5988" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:39:48</a> | Were shovels considered unclean or just expensive? | He said metal tools were expensive, so ordinary farmers often used their feet rather than hoes or shovels. | He said the issue was cost, not ritual uncleanness. Bronze or metal tools were expensive, so wealthy people might have a hoe, but ordinary farmers could simply use their feet to shape irrigation channels, and garden walkways could function as water channels. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=6131" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:42:11</a> | What does Mark 9:49 mean? | He said "salted with fire" draws on offering language and points to purification and works being tested by fire. | He read the phrase in the context of judgment imagery and sacrificial language. Since offerings were salted and then burned, "salted with fire" means deeds are treated like an offering and tested or judged by fire, with the result tied to purification and evaluation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=6540" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:00</a> | If the Levites start carrying the ark after Aaron's death in Deuteronomy 10:6-8, who carried it before? | He said the clan of Kohath carried it earlier, and the Deuteronomy passage is likely a scribal gloss reflecting later Levitical identity. | He said the Deuteronomy section looks parenthetical and includes "until this day," which marks it as likely scribal gloss material. Before the later generalized Levitical wording, the Kohathite clan had the duty of carrying the ark and tabernacle, though clan identities became less distinct after settlement in Levitical cities. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=6815" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:53:35</a> | What was the ancient Israelite perception of Sheol? | He said both righteous and unrighteous dead went to Sheol in the Old Testament, with later Second Temple thought developing a divided Sheol. | He said the Old Testament view places both righteous and unrighteous dead in Sheol, because before Christ they could not go directly into God's presence. In Second Temple and New Testament thought, Sheol becomes divided into a place for the righteous dead and a place for the unrighteous, with the righteous going to heaven after the crucifixion or resurrection. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=7157" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:59:17</a> | With the state of seminaries today, what hope is there for proper theological education, especially Old Testament and history? | He said it is increasingly difficult to find quality theological education, though Dallas Theological Seminary's THM Old Testament department may still be a decent option. | He said he did not have a satisfying answer because theological and seminary education have been lowering standards to attract students. He named Dallas Theological Seminary's THM Old Testament department as possibly still decent, but warned against the biblical exegesis department and described the broader trend as bleak. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=7578" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:06:18</a> | What is the best period of Doctor Who? | He prefers classic Doctor Who, especially the late 1960s through early 1980s, with the second through fourth Doctors as a high point. | He said he is a classic Doctor Who fan, especially the John Pertwee and Tom Baker eras. He placed the strongest run from the late 1960s through the early 1980s, with the second through fourth Doctors as the height, while being much less enthusiastic about later classic-era Doctors. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=7727" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:08:47</a> | How do we know what is a scribal gloss in the Old Testament? | He said glosses tend to be parenthetical explanations and often introduce anachronisms. | He gave two earmarks: a gloss often interrupts the flow with an explanatory parenthetical, and it may use terms or references that would not fit the original setting. Such comments help later readers understand the text, but the explanatory or anachronistic character can mark them as later scribal additions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=8035" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:13:55</a> | How do we know Zoan is Tanis? | He said Zoan is the Semitic name for Tanis. | He said Zoan is essentially the Semitic form used for Tanis, and later scribes used the city name known in their own period. In Numbers, the reference to Zoan is an anachronism because Zoan did not yet exist at the time being described, which supports reading the phrase as a later explanatory gloss. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=8231" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:17:11</a> | Could the writer have meant a rebuild of Hebron rather than the location itself? | He said he does not think so, because Hebron was not rebuilt in that way before Tanis. | He rejected the rebuild explanation because Hebron was conquered and occupied rather than abandoned and rebuilt in the relevant way. Even where ancient cities were rebuilt, they were often rebuilt by sections, and he said no such rebuilding before Tanis fits Hebron's history. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=8388" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:19:48</a> | Does Genesis 34:23 refer to two tribes becoming one? | He said no; clan cultures do not merge that way, and the passage is more like one group trying to absorb another. | He argued that clan and tribal societies do not simply merge into one tribe because clans preserve autonomy for protection and warfare. In Genesis 34, the proposal that the other group's property will become "ours" is not peaceful tribal coalescence but one group trying to absorb or dominate another. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=8570" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:22:50</a> | Did the Benjaminites eat pork? | He said yes, they raised and ate pork, partly because they were separated from the rest of the Israelites by the Jordan. | After clarifying the question wording, he answered that the Benjaminites did raise and eat pork. He attributed this partly to their separation from the rest of Israel by the Jordan and added that pork eating did occur among Israelites at times. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=8730" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:25:30</a> | Did the Benjaminites ever stop eating pork? | He said he does not think they stopped, and that they were still eating pork into the Roman period. | He said he does not think the Benjaminites ever fully stopped, since evidence still points to pork eating in the New Testament or Roman period. He distinguished this from the highland early Israelite pattern, where pig bones are generally absent, while noting that some pork remains also occur in Iron Age Jerusalem. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=8962" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:29:22</a> | Did people know how to fry food back then? | Yes. He said frying was more an Iron Age practice because it required a vessel suitable for hot oil. | He said people did know how to fry, but it was more characteristic of the Iron Age because it required a pan or vessel that could handle hot oil. That made frying less typical of earlier Bronze Age contexts and dependent on suitable metal cookware. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=9115" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:31:55</a> | What are some other double-offense judgments? | He gave David's adultery with Bathsheba and murder of Uriah as an example. | He identified David's sin with Bathsheba as an immediate example because it combined adultery with arranging Uriah's death. Nathan's parable then exposed the compounded offense, making it a case where one sin is joined to another and receives prophetic judgment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=9325" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:35:25</a> | Is there a book that views Deuteronomy alongside Exodus, Leviticus, and Numbers? | He said he does not know of one offhand, though the question is interesting. | He said he could not think of a book that directly collates Deuteronomy with Exodus, Leviticus, and Numbers by event detail and order. He acknowledged the comparison would be interesting, but did not offer a specific title from memory. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=9542" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:39:02</a> | Does Deuteronomy 10:4 clarify whether God or Moses wrote on the second tablets? | He said the second-tablet text remains ambiguous, because God may have dictated while Moses wrote. | He compared Exodus 34 and Deuteronomy 10 and said the first tablets are explicitly written by the finger of God, but the second tablets are less clear. Because "he wrote" can involve either personal writing or dictation through a scribe, he left open whether God wrote directly or Moses carved what God dictated. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=9722" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:42:02</a> | Did ancient Israelites put blood on the doorposts every Passover, or was that a one-time thing? | He said it was a one-time thing for the first Passover, not a repeated annual ritual. | He said the doorpost blood belonged to the first Passover in Egypt, when Israel left the houses the next morning and did not remain to clean them. Later Passover observance did not repeat that doorpost ritual, so he treated it as a one-time exodus sign rather than an annual practice. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=10082" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:48:02</a> | Did everyone write today? | He said he did not write that day because he was working on video-game modeling and rendering in Blender. | He answered personally that he had not written that day because he was doing video-game work instead. Specifically, he had been modeling and rendering in Blender right up to the livestream. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=10207" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:50:07</a> | Based on John 14:1-3, did the destination of believers after death change after the resurrection? | Yes. He said believers moved from Sheol to heaven after the resurrection, but the final destination is the new heavens and new earth. | He said John 14 uses wedding imagery: the groom prepares a house and returns for the bride. He connected that to the change after Christ's work, where the righteous no longer remain in Sheol as before, while also saying the ultimate destination is the renewed creation when Christ returns. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=10399" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:53:19</a> | What if the groom did not have the resources or knowledge to build a house? | He said then the groom could not get married, because providing a home was part of the marriage expectation. | He said a groom who could not provide a house could not proceed to marriage, because providing a home was part of proving he could support a family. If he could not build it himself, he could hire someone, and even poor families still expected some kind of home before marriage moved beyond betrothal. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=10790" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:59:50</a> | What do you think about the patriarchs and the fossils of camels? | He said camel domestication by the early second millennium BC is well established, so patriarchal camels should not be treated as a problem. | He said the argument that camels are an anachronistic problem for the patriarchs is badly outdated. In his view, camel domestication reaches back well before the patriarchal period, with fully domesticated and bred camels by the early second millennium BC and evidence even earlier. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=11151" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:05:51</a> | In what areas of Jericho did Kathleen Kenyon see evidence of destruction? | He said Garstang found the Late Bronze IIB destruction-layer fragment in the middle building, while Kenyon found Late Bronze IIB pottery. | He distinguished pottery from destruction-layer evidence. Garstang found the fragment of the Late Bronze IIB destruction layer in the middle building, while Kenyon and other excavations found Late Bronze IIB pottery across the site; he also clarified that Garstang misdated parts of the destruction evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=11751" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:15:51</a> | Did the Egyptians believe pyramids or obelisks channeled energy as alchemists claim? | No. He said obelisks were solar monuments, while pyramids functioned more like royal resurrection machinery, not energy channels. | He rejected the energy-channel claim. Obelisks were solar needles or monuments that cast shadows in a solar cultic context, while pyramids were tied to the king's afterlife role, with the king returning from the underworld to assist those buried around him, not to channel impersonal energy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=11971" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:19:31</a> | Are you a Thomist? | No. He said he is not a Thomist and never has been. | He answered briefly and directly that he is not a Thomist and never has been. He added only that he has opinions about Thomism but is not attracted to it as his own philosophical position. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=12037" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:20:37</a> | What is a Thomist? | He defined a Thomist as someone who follows the philosophy of Thomas Aquinas. | He defined a Thomist as someone who follows Thomas Aquinas's philosophy. He noted that many Roman Catholics and some evangelicals, including people connected with Southern Evangelical Seminary, have been influenced by Thomism, but he personally finds it deficient and not his view. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=12124" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:22:04</a> | Do only special Egyptians get tombs? | He said tombs depended on economic status: anyone who could afford one could have one, while the poor were usually buried in sand. | He said tomb access was mainly economic, not limited to a special class. Wealthier people, including priests, scribes, craftsmen, and upper-middle-class Egyptians, could pay for tombs of varying quality, while the very poor were buried in sand pits, which could actually preserve bodies well. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=12499" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:28:19</a> | Why do some churches completely disregard the Old Testament? | He said many pastors either do not understand it or treat it as obsolete, which he described as a kind of cultural Marcionism. | He gave two reasons: lack of understanding and the belief that the Old Testament is obsolete because Christians have the New Testament. He called this a form of Marcionite thinking and argued that the New Testament, including Hebrews, cannot be understood well without the Old Testament. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=13178" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:39:38</a> | Who wrote the book titled Biblical Archaeology next to Garstang's Story of Jericho on your shelf? | He identified it as a 1974 volume edited by Shalom Paul and William Dever, but recommended newer introductions instead. | He checked the shelf and identified the book as an older Biblical Archaeology volume edited by Shalom Paul and William Dever, published in 1974. He said he would not recommend it now because it is out of date, instead pointing to newer introductions such as Matthieu Richelle's work or his own fiction as an accessible archaeology introduction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=13482" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:44:42</a> | What do you think about Samaritan Pentateuch textual variants versus the Masoretic Text? | He said the Masoretic Text, Septuagint, and Samaritan Pentateuch are all important textual traditions for Pentateuchal criticism. | He said the Samaritan Pentateuch is one of the major ancient Pentateuchal traditions alongside the Masoretic Text and Septuagint. None of the three are identical, all are important for textual criticism, and he consults the Samaritan Pentateuch even though he does not treat it as uniquely original. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=13604" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:46:44</a> | If the battle of kings was in Hammurabi's 39th year, would we know the king of Elam? | He said no, because Elamite king lists and reign lengths are too uncertain, with multiple possible candidates. | He said the Elamite king lists are too vague to identify the king securely, since reign lengths, succession, and exact dates are uncertain. Around Hammurabi's thirty-ninth year there may be multiple similarly named candidates, and he said better Elamite and Mitanni king lists would be needed to resolve the chronology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=13880" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:51:20</a> | Why is Kip involved in the Exodus and conquest debate if his expertise is in Dead Sea Scrolls? | He dismissed the involvement as ideologically driven and not worth engaging. | He answered polemically, saying the involvement is driven by hostility to the Exodus and conquest rather than useful expertise. He said he no longer pays attention to those critics because he regards their engagement as ideological, repetitive, and not worth the time. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=14094" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:54:54</a> | What do you think of the claim that Yahweh's original nature was as a solar deity rather than a storm god? | He rejected both labels, saying Yahweh controls nature but is not reducible to a solar or storm deity. | He said the solar-deity argument often leans on iconography such as the winged disk on Hezekiah's seal, but that symbol could represent deity broadly rather than a specifically solar god. He rejected both solar and storm labels, saying Yahweh controls nature but is not identified with the storm, sun, thunder, fire, or lightning. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=14548" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:02:28</a> | Would Moses have used Ivri script or Proto-Sinaitic script? | He said Moses probably used an alphabetic script, likely some kind of Proto-Sinaitic or early alphabetic form, rather than Egyptian. | He interpreted "Ivri" as Paleo-Hebrew or an early Hebrew script and said there is not a huge difference from Proto-Sinaitic at that early stage. Because Moses lived among a heavily Semitic community where alphabetic scripts were available, he thought an early alphabetic or Proto-Sinaitic-type script was more likely than Egyptian. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=14935" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:08:55</a> | Is there new evidence for a pre-Fourth Dynasty Sphinx? | He said minor sphinxes could potentially be earlier, but he strongly rejected claims that the main Great Sphinx predates the Fourth Dynasty. | He allowed that minor sphinxes or sphinx-related monuments might conceivably predate the Fourth Dynasty, though he had not seen the specific evidence. But for the main Great Sphinx, he said the contextual data tie it to the Fourth Dynasty and Khafre's complex so strongly that he rejects pre-Fourth-Dynasty claims. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Gjz-v5Wi804?t=15257" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:14:17</a> | Have we barely scratched the surface of ancient Egypt archaeology because of the Egyptian government? | He said the reasons include government gatekeeping, funding, institutional affiliation, and local control over promising sites. | He said the Egyptian government is one factor because it tends to reserve the best sites for Egyptian nationals or connected local projects. But he also emphasized funding and institutional affiliation: archaeology is expensive, and permit decisions weigh whether a team has the money, whether the site is promising, and who should get access to it. |
